name: Bibi Harnam Kaur
role: The Well-Listener · hydrologist of the Faltering, architect of the Panj Paani Ahd
quote: Everyone asked me how to get more water. Wrong question. The question was how to become the kind of people a river could trust.
note: She spent the drought years lowering microphones into dying wells and making politicians listen to the silence on loudspeakers. The treaty tent on the dry riverbed was her idea: 'Let the negotiators sit where the water should be.'

name: Fateh Singh Aulakh
role: Langar-grid engineer · first weaver of the kitchen mesh
quote: I did not invent anything. The langar was already a grid — five hundred years of load-balancing. I just added wires to a wisdom.
note: A power engineer who lost his farm to the debt years and rebuilt his vocation in gurdwara kitchens. His founding schematic for the Langar Grid is drawn on the back of a degh requisition form, now in the Salt Archive.

name: Iqbal Bano Sandhu
role: First head gardener of Sanjha Bagh · keeper of kilometre zero
quote: People say the garden healed the border. No. The border was never sick — the border was the sickness. We composted it.
note: Granddaughter of Partition survivors from both directions. She instituted the vow of two scripts and personally planted the bench at kilometre zero, under the descendant of the original wire-grown vine.

name: Roop Lal Sahota
role: Balladeer of the canopy roads · voice of the Sunfield Covenant
quote: They etched the names of the debt-dead into the pylon and asked me to sing something hopeful. I sang the names. Hope that skips the grief is just forgetting with a melody.
note: Born landless in the old cotton tracts, he walked every kilometre of the canopy fields singing the covenant villages into the trust, one panchayat at a time. His song-cycle 'Chhaan Vando' is still sung at Vaisakhi before any other music.

name: Sundar Kaur Cheema
role: Third trustee of the Glacier Trust · author of the Children's Renewal
quote: Adults kept signing fifty-year promises with thirty years left in them. I simply matched the signature to the lifespan. The children were never the symbolic choice. They were the legal one.
note: A water-rights lawyer who took the rotation at the headwaters and never fully came down. Her ruling on the Children's Renewal runs four pages; the dissent she wrote to her own ruling, anticipating its abuses, runs forty.

name: Agam
role: First child-signatory of the renewed Glacier Compact
quote: I was eleven. I remember the cold of the water in my hand, and the keeper saying 'drink facing downstream.' I have made every decision of my life facing downstream since.
note: History records only the single name, by Agam's own lifelong insistence: 'Four thousand of us climbed. I held the pen for a minute.' Later served sixty years as a snow surveyor and declined the trusteeship twice.
